schools.summary: Paul's tender, emphatic charge: 'thou therefore, my son, be strong (endynamou — be strengthened, gain strength, be empowered) in the grace that is in Christ Jesus' — drawing the conclusion from his own example and the defections (ch.1); the passive form ('be strengthened') showing the strength is not self-derived but received, Timothy not summoning strength from himself but empowered by grace, the strength coming from God (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Chrysostom: the strength is 'in the grace that is in Christ Jesus' — the grace that flows from and resides in Christ; union with Christ is the believer's reservoir of strength, from which the minister draws for his task.

themes: Thou therefore, my son, be strong in the grace that is in Christ Jesus
the charge to be strong in grace
strengthened, not self-derived
grace in Christ the source

schools.summary: The doctrine to be transmitted: 'the things that thou hast heard of me among many witnesses' — the apostolic teaching Timothy received from Paul, attested publicly before many, the sound doctrine openly delivered not secret; 'the same commit (paratheme — entrust, as a deposit) thou to faithful (trustworthy, reliable) men' — Timothy to hand on the deposit to faithful men who will hold it true, the trust passing through reliable hands (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the men chosen must be 'able to teach others also' — not only faithful to hold the truth but competent to pass it on; the succession of sound teaching is secured through men both trustworthy and apt to teach, a chain of faithful transmission.

themes: the same commit thou to faithful men, who shall be able to teach others also
the things that thou hast heard of me among many witnesses
entrusting the truth to faithful teachers
the succession of sound doctrine

schools.summary: The first of three figures (soldier, athlete, farmer) Paul uses for the minister: 'thou therefore endure hardness (synkakopatheson — suffer hardship, endure affliction together with me) as a good soldier of Jesus Christ' — Timothy to bear his sufferings like a soldier on campaign; the soldier expects and endures hardship (toil, danger, privation) without complaint, the minister of Christ, enlisted under him as his captain in a spiritual warfare, likewise ready to suffer for the cause, loyal and brave (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Thou therefore endure hardness, as a good soldier of Jesus Christ
the call to endure hardness as a soldier
bearing hardship for Christ
the good soldier of Christ

schools.summary: The point of the soldier figure: 'no man that warreth (serves as a soldier on active duty) entangleth himself with the affairs (the business, pursuits) of this life' — the soldier on campaign sets aside ordinary civilian occupations to give himself wholly to his service; the lesson for the minister being that, like the soldier, he is not to be entangled in worldly business and cares that would distract from his calling, undivided devotion required; 'that he may please him who hath chosen (enlisted) him to be a soldier' — the one aim to please his commander, Christ (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley).

themes: No man that warreth entangleth himself with the affairs of this life
that he may please him who hath chosen him to be a soldier
the soldier's freedom from worldly entanglement
undivided devotion to please Christ

schools.summary: The second figure (the athlete): 'and if a man also strive for masteries (athle — competes as an athlete in the games), yet is he not crowned (does not receive the victor's wreath), except he strive (compete) lawfully' — the athlete wins the crown only by competing according to the rules of the contest (keeping the course, the wrestler's rules); the lesson being that the minister must serve according to God's rules, faithfulness in the appointed way (not mere effort) winning the reward, zeal unregulated by truth and obedience gaining no crown (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: if a man also strive for masteries, yet is he not crowned, except he strive lawfully
the athlete crowned only if he strives lawfully
serving by God's rules
faithfulness, not mere effort, wins the crown

schools.summary: The third figure (the farmer): 'the husbandman (farmer) that laboureth (toils, works hard) must be first partaker of the fruits' — the farmer who works the hardest has the first and rightful claim to the harvest, labour preceding and earning the enjoyment of the fruit; so the minister who labours faithfully will partake of the fruits of his labour (whether the present comfort of seeing fruit, or the future reward); the three figures together (soldier, athlete, farmer) teaching that reward follows endurance, discipline, and toil (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: The husbandman that laboureth must be first partaker of the fruits
the labouring farmer first to partake
toil before reward
the minister sharing the fruits of his labour

schools.summary: Paul's exhortation following the three figures: 'consider (noei — think over, ponder, attend to) what I say' — Timothy to reflect carefully on the lessons of the soldier, athlete, and farmer to grasp their full meaning; 'and the Lord give (grant) thee understanding (synesis — insight, comprehension) in all things' — even reflection needs divine help, true insight being God's gift not the fruit of human thought alone; the union of human diligence and divine illumination, we ponder but God gives the insight (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Consider what I say; and the Lord give thee understanding in all things
the call to consider with God giving understanding
pondering and divine illumination
human diligence and divine grace

schools.summary: Paul presses the gospel's core: 'remember (keep in mind, hold fast) that Jesus Christ... was raised from the dead' — the resurrection the heart and pledge of the gospel, the ground of hope and endurance, ever to be kept before Timothy; 'of the seed of David' — Christ's true humanity and his descent from David, fulfilling the messianic promise, the risen Lord both true man and promised Messiah; 'according to my gospel (the gospel I preach)' — the risen, Davidic Christ the sum of the gospel Paul proclaimed (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).
Cyril: the risen Christ of David's seed is the substance of the gospel; Timothy's preaching and his endurance alike rest on this central fact, that the true man and Messiah has conquered death.

themes: Remember that Jesus Christ of the seed of David was raised from the dead
according to my gospel
the risen Christ of David's seed
the heart of the gospel

schools.summary: Paul's testimony: 'wherein (in which gospel) I suffer trouble (hardship, affliction), as an evil doer (as if I were a criminal), even unto bonds (chains, imprisonment)' — for preaching this gospel Paul is treated as a malefactor and chained like a common criminal, the indignity of being so treated though he had done only good, borne for Christ; 'but the word of God is not bound (not chained, not imprisoned)' — though the preacher is in chains, the gospel runs free (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Chrysostom: no chains can imprison the truth; though Paul is bound, the word of God continues to spread and conquer, running free through the world — the triumph of the gospel over the bonds of its preacher.

themes: I suffer trouble, as an evil doer, even unto bonds; but the word of God is not bound
Paul bound for the gospel
the word of God not bound
the gospel running free though the preacher is chained

schools.summary: Paul's purpose in suffering: 'therefore I endure all things for the elect's sakes (for the sake of the chosen, God's people)' — his sufferings not for himself but for the good of those God has chosen, bearing all that they may be saved; 'that they may also obtain (gain, attain) the salvation which is in Christ Jesus with eternal glory' — the end their salvation, crowned with eternal glory, Paul's sufferings furthering the elect's attainment of salvation and the glory that follows (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Gill): 'the elect' are those God has chosen to salvation; Paul's labours and sufferings serve the gathering and salvation of God's elect, whom the gospel infallibly reaches and saves — his endurance is in the service of God's electing purpose.

themes: I endure all things for the elect's sakes
that they may also obtain the salvation which is in Christ Jesus with eternal glory
enduring for the elect's salvation
salvation crowned with eternal glory

schools.summary: The trustworthy-word formula (as 1 Timothy 1:15; 3:1): 'it is a faithful saying (a trustworthy word)' — introducing a rhythmic, perhaps hymnic or creedal saying that Paul affirms as sure, possibly a fragment of an early Christian hymn; 'for if we be dead with him (died with him, in union with his death, whether dying to sin or dying for him in martyrdom), we shall also live with him' — death with Christ leads to life with him, union with his death securing union with his life (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Chrysostom: the dying believer, sharing Christ's death (in baptism, in mortification, in suffering), is assured of sharing his risen life; the saying steadies the suffering minister with the certainty of life beyond death.

themes: It is a faithful saying: For if we be dead with him, we shall also live with him
the faithful saying
dying with Christ, living with him
union with his death securing his life

schools.summary: The next pair of the faithful saying: 'if we suffer (endure, hold out under affliction), we shall also reign with him' — enduring suffering with Christ leads to reigning with him, present suffering the path to future glory and royal reign; 'if we deny (disown, renounce) him, he also will deny us' — those who disown Christ (as Peter feared, or the apostate) will be disowned by him (cf. Matthew 10:33), the warning balancing the promise, deterring from apostasy under persecution, the saying setting reward and penalty side by side (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: If we suffer, we shall also reign with him: if we deny him, he also will deny us
the promise to those who suffer and the warning to those who deny
suffering the path to reigning
the warning against apostasy

schools.summary: The close of the faithful saying: 'if we believe not (are faithless, unfaithful), yet he abideth faithful (remains true, trustworthy)' — even human unfaithfulness cannot make Christ unfaithful, his faithfulness not depending on ours; this cuts both ways — he is faithful to his promises (to save the believing) and faithful to his warnings (to judge the denier of v.12), faithful to his own word and character; 'he cannot deny (be false to) himself' — his faithfulness flowing from his unchangeable nature, unable to act against his own character and word, for to be unfaithful would be to deny himself, which is impossible (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: If we believe not, yet he abideth faithful: he cannot deny himself
Christ abiding faithful when we are faithless
faithful to promise and warning
he cannot deny himself

schools.summary: Paul's charge to Timothy's teaching: 'of these things (the great truths just stated) put them in remembrance (remind the people)' — keeping the saving truths before the church; 'charging (solemnly testifying) them before the Lord (in God's sight) that they strive not about words (logomachein — wage word-battles, dispute about mere words)' — solemnly warning against the empty verbal contentions of the false teachers; 'to no profit, but to the subverting (katastrophe — overturning, ruin) of the hearers' — such strife useless and positively harmful, overthrowing the faith of those who listen (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: charging them before the Lord that they strive not about words to no profit
but to the subverting of the hearers
the charge against unprofitable word-strife
word-battles that ruin the hearers

schools.summary: Paul's positive charge over against word-strife: 'study (spoudason — be diligent, give diligence, make every effort) to shew (present) thyself approved (dokimon — tested and approved, as metal proved genuine) unto God' — Timothy to labour to be approved by God not men; 'a workman (labourer) that needeth not to be ashamed' — like a skilled craftsman whose work bears inspection, the faithful minister having no cause for shame when his labour is examined; 'rightly dividing (orthotomeo — cutting straight, handling rightly, as cutting a straight path or rightly portioning) the word of truth' — handling Scripture correctly, dividing it aright, giving each hearer his due portion (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Study to shew thyself approved unto God, a workman that needeth not to be ashamed
rightly dividing the word of truth
the approved workman
rightly dividing the word

schools.summary: Paul's command over against rightly dividing the word: 'but shun (periistaso — avoid, turn away from, give a wide berth to) profane (unholy, irreligious) and vain (empty, idle) babblings (kenophonias — empty chatter, foolish talk)' — Timothy to keep clear of the empty, irreligious talk of the false teachers, the empty speculations and disputes of the errorists, idle and unprofitable; 'for they will increase (advance, make progress) unto more ungodliness (impiety)' — such babblings not staying harmless but growing worse, error being progressive, spreading and deepening ungodliness (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: But shun profane and vain babblings: for they will increase unto more ungodliness
the charge to shun profane babblings
empty talk breeding ungodliness
error progressive

schools.summary: The vivid image: 'and their word (the teaching of the errorists) will eat (nomen hexei — have pasture, spread, consume) as doth a canker (gangraina — gangrene, a spreading, eating sore)' — false teaching spreads like gangrene, eating away the healthy flesh, consuming all it touches; gangrene spreads relentlessly, destroying living tissue and proving fatal if not cut off, so error if unchecked spreads through the church, corrupting and destroying, and must be stopped not tolerated; 'of whom is Hymenaeus and Philetus' — two named teachers of this error (Hymenaeus also in 1 Timothy 1:20), concrete examples (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: their word will eat as doth a canker: of whom is Hymenaeus and Philetus
the spreading corruption of error
false teaching like gangrene
Hymenaeus and Philetus named

schools.summary: The description of the errorists: 'who concerning the truth have erred (astochesan — missed the mark, swerved, gone astray)' — Hymenaeus and Philetus had wandered from the truth; 'saying that the resurrection is past already' — they taught that the resurrection had already occurred, spiritualizing it (taking it as merely the new birth or a moral/spiritual rising already accomplished) and so denying the future bodily resurrection of believers, an over-realized, spiritualized eschatology; 'and overthrow (anatrepousin — overturn, subvert) the faith of some' — the error doing real harm, upsetting and destroying the faith of those who embraced it, cutting at the root of Christian hope (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: who concerning the truth have erred, saying that the resurrection is past already
and overthrow the faith of some
the error that the resurrection is past
a spiritualized resurrection overthrowing faith

schools.summary: The comfort against the errorists' damage: 'nevertheless the foundation of God standeth sure (stands firm, is unshaken)' — though some be overthrown, God's foundation (whether his church, his elect, his truth, or his eternal purpose) remains firm and immovable; 'having this seal' (the figure of a foundation-stone bearing an inscription), with two sides: 'The Lord knoweth (recognizes, owns) them that are his' — God's side, he infallibly knows and owns his own, the elect secure however error rages; 'and, Let every one that nameth the name of Christ (professes to be his) depart from (renounce, forsake) iniquity' — the human side, professors proving their claim by holiness (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: the foundation of God standeth sure, having this seal, The Lord knoweth them that are his
Let every one that nameth the name of Christ depart from iniquity
God's sure foundation with its twofold seal
God knows his own; let his own depart from iniquity

schools.summary: The illustration of the church (or the visible professing body): 'but in a great house (a large mansion) there are not only vessels (utensils) of gold and of silver (precious, for noble use), but also of wood and of earth (common, cheap); and some to honour (for honourable, noble use), and some to dishonour (for base, menial use)' — so in the church there are members of differing character, some honourable (true and useful), some dishonourable (false or unworthy); the mixed state of the visible church, the presence of unworthy vessels (the errorists) no cause for surprise, since even a great house has common and base vessels alongside the precious (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).

themes: in a great house there are not only vessels of gold and of silver, but also of wood and of earth
some to honour, and some to dishonour
the great house with varied vessels
the mixed state of the visible church

schools.summary: The application of the vessel figure: 'if a man therefore purge (ekkathare — cleanse, purify) himself from these (from the dishonourable vessels, the errorists and their corruptions)' — by separating from the false and impure a man cleanses himself; 'he shall be a vessel unto honour (for noble use), sanctified (set apart, made holy), and meet (useful, fit) for the master's use, and prepared (made ready) unto every good work' — the one who purges himself becoming a vessel of honour, consecrated for God's service, fit for the Master's hand and ready for every good service (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: If a man therefore purge himself from these, he shall be a vessel unto honour
sanctified, and meet for the master's use, and prepared unto every good work
the call to purge oneself to be a vessel unto honour
cleansed, consecrated, and useful

schools.summary: Paul's charge to the young minister: 'flee (pheuge — run from, escape) also youthful lusts (the desires and passions of youth — not only sensual, but pride, ambition, impatience, contentiousness, love of novelty)' — Timothy to run from the characteristic temptations of his age; 'but follow (pursue, chase after) righteousness (uprightness), faith, charity (love), peace' — over against fleeing lusts, pursuing the cluster of graces, flight from evil joined with pursuit of good; 'with them that call on the Lord (true believers, worshippers) out of a pure heart' — in the fellowship of sincere believers, the graces pursued together with the pure-hearted people of God (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: Flee also youthful lusts: but follow righteousness, faith, charity, peace
with them that call on the Lord out of a pure heart
fleeing youthful lusts and following righteousness
pursuing grace with the pure-hearted

schools.summary: Paul's command: 'but foolish (silly, senseless) and unlearned (uninstructed, ignorant, undisciplined) questions (speculations, debates) avoid (paraitou — refuse, decline, have nothing to do with)' — Timothy to refuse to engage in the silly, ignorant disputes of the false teachers, the idle speculations and controversies of the errorists (foolish in being pointless, unlearned in springing from ignorance not true knowledge); 'knowing that they do gender (beget, breed, give birth to) strifes (quarrels, contentions)' — such questions producing nothing but quarrels, the certain fruit being contention not edification (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: But foolish and unlearned questions avoid, knowing that they do gender strifes
the charge to avoid foolish questions
idle speculations that breed strife
refusing strife-breeding debates

schools.summary: The character of the true minister: 'and the servant (bondservant) of the Lord must not strive (machesthai — quarrel, wrangle, fight)' — not contentious or quarrelsome, strife ill-becoming Christ's servant whatever the provocation; 'but be gentle (epion — mild, kind) unto all men, apt to teach (didaktikon — skilled and ready to teach), patient (anexikakon — forbearing, bearing evil without resentment, enduring wrong)' — over against strife, the minister to be gentle to all, able to teach, and forbearing under provocation, bearing patiently with the difficult, the opposing, and the ignorant (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley).

themes: the servant of the Lord must not strive; but be gentle unto all men, apt to teach, patient
the servant marked by gentleness, not strife
mildness, teaching skill, and forbearance
the spirit of the true minister

schools.summary: The spirit of correction: 'in meekness (gentleness) instructing (correcting, schooling) those that oppose themselves (antidiatithemenous — set themselves in opposition to the truth)' — those in error corrected not with anger or denunciation but with patient gentleness, the wisest as well as the kindest course (Barnes notes you will never convince a man you are right by making him angry); 'if God peradventure will give (grant) them repentance to the acknowledging (full knowledge, recognition) of the truth' — most (Ellicott, Gill, the common reading) take repentance as God's gift, granted by him, the minister instructing but the change of heart being God's to give, leading to embracing and confessing the truth.

Wesley: repentance here is 'wholly his work' — God's gift, which the minister can only hope God may grant; Clarke: he is to use every means God may bless, for though bad, the opposers are not beyond God's mercy. Even the Arminian voices own repentance as the gift of God.

Barnes dissents on the grammar: he holds the Greek mepote refers not to God's giving but to the opposers' posture — they set themselves in opposition 'lest' they be brought to repentance, the deliberate self-hardening of those who will not repent; the uniform sense of mepote elsewhere ('lest at any time') favouring this, though he notes all the critics take it of God's giving.

debates.question: Does 'if God peradventure will give them repentance' make repentance God's gift?

sides.position: Yes — repentance is the gift of God, granted by him (even Wesley: 'wholly his work')
The grammar (mepote) rather describes the opposers' deliberate self-hardening 'lest' they repent

themes: In meekness instructing those that oppose themselves
if God peradventure will give them repentance to the acknowledging of the truth
instructing opposers in meekness
repentance as God's gift

schools.summary: The further aim of the gentle instruction: 'and that they may recover themselves (ananepso — return to soberness, come to their senses, awake as from a drunken stupor) out of the snare (trap, net) of the devil' — the hope that the opposers may be freed from the trap in which Satan has caught them, error and opposition to the truth being a 'snare of the devil' from which they need deliverance; 'who are taken captive by him (the devil) at his will (to do his will)' — held captive by Satan to serve his purposes (though some refer the pronouns differently, taken captive by the servant of the Lord to do God's will, most take it as captured by the devil to do the devil's will) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Calvin.

themes: that they may recover themselves out of the snare of the devil
who are taken captive by him at his will
the hope of recovery from the devil's snare
freed from Satan's trap by repentance
